metadata.description: Retrospective logging: citation style change from author-prominent to information-prominent across manuscript, plus precision fixes (95% to 95.5%, 4.6% to 4.5%)

summary: Retrospective logging of two decisions: (1) Citation style change from author-prominent to information-prominent for 15 citations, eliminating all 3 literature-chain paragraphs, reducing AP ratio from 47% to 28%. (2) Precision standardization: 95% to 95.5% in 5 instances, 4.6% to 4.5% in 2 instances. Both changes applied to NMS2026 and NatureCollection2026 manuscripts (all 4 versions + docx files).

decision.description: Convert 15 author-prominent citations to information-prominent style across the manuscript. Author-prominent ('Zai et al. (2025) found...') places the author name as grammatical subject. Information-prominent ('Public AI discourse gives disproportionate standing to institutional actors (Zai et al., 2025)') places the idea first and cites parenthetically. This eliminates literature-chain paragraphs where 4+ consecutive sentences each begin with 'Author (Year) verb...' Retain author-prominent only where the author's identity is integral to the argument (Entman's framework, Baeyaert's theoretical contribution, papers engaged with in depth like Bishop, Malecki, Campos Valverde).
decision.rationale: Literature-chain paragraphs (Author found X. Author found Y. Author found Z.) are a visible AI-generated writing pattern and read like a mechanical literature review rather than an argument-driven empirical paper. Information-prominent citation lets ideas lead and evidence follow, which is more natural for social science writing and better serves readers who care about the claims, not who made them. Author-prominent remains appropriate when introducing a named theoretical framework or engaging deeply with a single paper's findings.
decision.revision_note: Accepted and implemented. User directed the change after reviewing the analysis of author-prominent vs information-prominent citation patterns. 15 citations converted. Literature chains eliminated (from 3 chains to 0). Author-prominent ratio reduced from 47% to 28%.

context.conversation_snippet: User: 'it seems like it would read much cleaner, flow better, and feel more natural or human if we went the latter way [information-prominent]. can you investigate this and give me your suggestions?'

contribution.description: Converted 15 author-prominent citations to information-prominent across Introduction (lines 37, 39) and Discussion (lines 234, 244, 264, 274). Eliminated all 3 literature-chain paragraphs. Retained 19 author-prominent citations where author identity is integral (Entman, Baeyaert, Bishop, Malecki, Campos Valverde, Ferrari, DiMaggio). Applied to both NMS2026 and NatureCollection2026 manuscripts (both anonymous and non-anonymous versions). Regenerated all docx files.

context.conversation_snippet: User: 'Yes, implement these changes that you described and then rescore the naturalness score'

decision.description: Standardize all finding-related compression percentages to precise values: use 95.5% consistently (not 95%), and change 4.6% to 4.5% to match arithmetic (100 - 95.5 = 4.5). Retain standalone 95% only in the Methods section where it refers to a coverage threshold parameter, not the compression finding.
decision.rationale: Precision and internal consistency. The individual cluster percentages (40.6 + 21.3 + 18.7 + 14.9) sum to exactly 95.5%, so the remaining must be 4.5%. Using rounded 95% in some places and precise 95.5% in others, and stating 4.6% when the arithmetic yields 4.5%, creates unnecessary inconsistency that a careful reviewer would notice.
decision.revision_note: Accepted and implemented. Changed 5 instances of rounded '95%' to precise '95.5%' and 2 instances of '4.6%' to '4.5%' across all manuscript versions. The one remaining '95%' is in the Methods section referring to a coverage threshold parameter, which is correct as-is.

context.conversation_snippet: User: 'instead of saying 95% sometimes and 95.5% other times, make sure we always use 95.5% everywhere. also, will it change anything to change the 4.6% to 4.5%?'
